g.text: Cache memories are small, fast memory locations that store frequently accessed data or instructions.
The cache memory hierarchy consists of multiple levels, each with a specific size and access time.

text.data-narration: Level 1 (L1) cache: Smallest and fastest cache, located on the CPU.
Level 2 (L2) cache: Larger and slower cache, located on the CPU or on a separate chip.
Level 3 (L3) cache: Largest and slowest cache, located on a separate chip.
Direct Mapping: each memory address is mapped to a specific location in the cache memory.
Indirect Mapping: multiple memory addresses are mapped to the same location in the cache memory.
Set-Associative Mapping: multiple memory addresses are mapped to the same location in the cache memory, but each memory address has a unique identifier (set).

Proportional-Associative Mapping: multiple memory addresses are mapped to the same location in the cache memory, but each memory address has a unique identifier (set) and each set has a limited number of cache lines.

Full-Associative Mapping: multiple memory addresses are mapped to the same location in the cache memory, and each memory address has a unique identifier (set) and each set has a limited number of cache lines.
Cache Misses: when the CPU requests a memory address that is not in the cache memory.
Cache Hits: when the CPU requests a memory address that is in the cache memory.
